posts.title: State Space Models: Attention's Linear-Time Rival
posts.subtitle: Mamba compresses an entire sequence into one fixed-size state, trading a little exact recall for constant-memory inference at any context length.
posts.summary: State Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba process sequences in linear time by compressing history into a fixed-size hidden state instead of attending over every past token. Mamba's selective mechanism makes that compression input-dependent — the model chooses what to remember per token — and a hardware-aware parallel scan keeps training fast on GPUs, at the cost of weaker exact long-range recall than full attention.

slides.title: What If a Model Never Had to Look Back?
slides.body: Self-attention re-reads every previous token at every step. State space models instead carry one running summary forward — like a hidden state in an RNN, but engineered to train in parallel and remember selectively.
bullets: Core promise: process a sequence in O(n) time instead of attention's O(n²)
Popularized by Mamba (Gu & Dao) and used in hybrids like Jamba and Nemotron-H
Inference memory stays constant per step — no growing KV cache

slides.title: Attention's Quadratic Bill Comes Due at Long Context
slides.body: Every token in a Transformer attends to every other token, so both compute and the KV cache grow with context length — quadratically for compute, linearly per token for cache memory that keeps accumulating.
bullets: A 200K-token context means a KV cache that keeps growing the whole conversation
Classic RNNs are O(n) and constant-memory, but train sequentially and forget over long ranges
The gap SSMs try to close: RNN-like efficiency without RNN-like training and memory problems

slides.title: Selective State: Choosing What to Remember, Per Token
slides.body: Earlier SSMs (like S4) had fixed, input-independent dynamics — the same decay/update rule regardless of content. Mamba's key change is making those dynamics selective: the update depends on the current token, so the model can let irrelevant tokens pass through unchanged and let important ones overwrite the state.
bullets: S4-style SSMs: linear time-invariant — same update rule every step
Mamba: input-dependent (selective) update — content decides what's kept or dropped
This selectivity is what let SSMs finally compete with attention on language modeling quality

slides.title: Trains Like a Scan, Runs Inference Like an RNN
slides.body: The recurrence looks sequential, but a hardware-aware parallel scan (plus kernel fusion that avoids materializing large intermediates in GPU memory) lets Mamba train in parallel across the sequence, closing much of the throughput gap with attention.
bullets: Training: parallel scan algorithm processes the whole sequence at once on GPU
Inference: recurrent mode — one fixed-size state updated per new token
No KV cache to grow: memory per generated token stays flat regardless of context length

slides.title: Attention vs Selective State Space, Side by Side
slides.body: Neither is strictly better — they trade compute pattern for recall precision.

slides.title: Streaming a 500K-Token Sensor Log Without the Memory Blowup
slides.body: An agent ingesting a continuous 500K-token log stream with a Transformer would carry a KV cache that keeps growing for the whole session. A Mamba-style layer instead keeps one fixed-size hidden state updated per token — memory per step never grows, which is why production hybrids like Jamba (AI21) and Nvidia's Nemotron-H interleave Mamba layers with a handful of attention layers for exactly this workload.
bullets: Jamba: alternates Mamba and attention blocks in one model
Nemotron-H: mostly Mamba-2 layers with sparse attention layers mixed in
Goal of hybrids: keep SSM's flat memory, borrow attention's precise recall where it matters most

slides.title: When to Reach for an SSM — and When Not To
slides.body: SSMs win on throughput and memory at long context; attention still wins when you need to pinpoint one exact fact from far back.
bullets: Use SSM/hybrid layers for: very long or streaming context, cheap constant-memory inference
Stick with full attention for: short-context tasks needing precise 'needle in a haystack' recall
Pure-SSM models tend to underperform on exact associative recall — most production systems hybridize
Not a drop-in replacement everywhere: pick per workload, not by default

questions.prompt: What is Mamba's key innovation over earlier state space models like S4?
options: It removes the hidden state entirely
It makes the state-transition dynamics input-dependent (selective) instead of fixed
It adds more attention heads
It only works on images
questions.explanation: S4-style SSMs use the same linear time-invariant update at every step regardless of content. Mamba makes that update depend on the current input, so the model can selectively remember or forget per token.

questions.prompt: Why does per-token inference memory stay flat for an SSM but keep growing for attention?
options: SSMs don't process all tokens
Attention needs a KV cache that grows with every new token in context, while an SSM updates one fixed-size state
Attention uses less memory in general
SSMs store the entire input as raw text
questions.explanation: Attention must keep every past token's key/value vectors around to attend to them later, so the KV cache grows with context length. An SSM instead compresses all history into one fixed-size hidden state that gets updated, not extended.

questions.prompt: Why do production models like Jamba and Nemotron-H mix Mamba layers with a few attention layers instead of using pure SSM?
options: To make the model slower on purpose
Because pure SSMs can't run on GPUs
To recover attention's precise long-range recall while keeping most of SSM's memory and speed advantage
Attention layers are required by law for AI safety
questions.explanation: Pure SSMs compress history into a fixed state and can underperform on tasks needing exact recall of a specific far-back token. Hybrids interleave a handful of attention layers to patch that weakness while keeping most of the SSM efficiency.

references.title: Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces (arXiv:2312.00752)

posts.title: SkillOpt: Training the Skill Doc, Not the Model
posts.subtitle: Microsoft Research's text-space optimizer edits a single skill file until it wins on 52 out of 52 benchmark cells — with zero extra inference cost.
posts.summary: SkillOpt (arXiv:2605.23904, Microsoft Research with SJTU/Tongji/Fudan) treats an agent's natural-language skill document as the thing you optimize, not the model weights: a separate optimizer model proposes bounded edits from scored rollouts, and an edit only survives if it strictly improves a held-out validation score. Across 6 benchmarks, 7 target models, and 3 execution harnesses, it was best-or-tied on all 52 cells, lifting GPT-5.5's average accuracy by up to +24.8 points with zero added inference-time cost.

slides.title: What If the Skill File Were the Thing You Trained?
slides.body: Posted to arXiv in May 2026 (arXiv:2605.23904), SkillOpt comes from Microsoft Research with SJTU, Tongji, and Fudan. It treats a frozen agent's natural-language skill document as external, trainable state — optimized with the same discipline as weight-space training, but without touching a single weight.
bullets: arXiv:2605.23904 — 'SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills'
Core idea: a separate optimizer model edits a skill markdown file based on scored rollouts
Headline result: best-or-tied on all 52 of 52 (model, benchmark, harness) cells tested

slides.title: Today's Agent Skills Are Written Once and Hoped For
slides.body: Agent 'skills' — the instructions/playbooks an agent reads before acting — are usually hand-written, generated once by an LLM, or loosely self-revised. None of that behaves like a real optimizer: there's no guarantee a revision actually helps, and no principled way to know when to stop.
bullets: Hand-crafted skills don't adapt to where the agent is actually failing
One-shot LLM-generated skills are never checked against a validation signal
Loose self-revision can drift the skill worse over time with no accept/reject gate

slides.title: Bounded Edits, Accepted Only If They Beat a Held-Out Score
slides.body: SkillOpt runs the agent, scores the rollouts, and has a separate optimizer model propose bounded add/delete/replace edits to a single skill document. An edit is kept only when it strictly improves a held-out validation score — otherwise it's discarded.
bullets: Optimizer model turns scored rollouts into small, bounded edits — not full rewrites
Validation-gated updates: an edit ships only if it strictly beats the current held-out score
Zero inference-time overhead — the trained artifact is just a markdown file the frozen agent reads

slides.title: Three Mechanisms That Keep Text Optimization Stable
slides.body: Editing a text document by gradient-like feedback can easily thrash. SkillOpt borrows stability tricks from weight-space optimization and reimplements them in text.
bullets: A textual 'learning-rate' budget caps how much a skill document can change per round
A rejected-edit buffer remembers edits that failed validation so they aren't retried
Epoch-wise slow/meta updates smooth out noisy single-rollout signal before committing a change

slides.title: Best-or-Tied on Every Cell, Against Six Prior Approaches
slides.body: The paper evaluates 6 benchmarks (including SpreadsheetBench, OfficeQA, and LiveMathematicianBench) across 7 target models and 3 execution harnesses (direct chat, Codex, Claude Code) — 52 total cells.

slides.title: The Numbers, and a Skill That Moved Between Tools
slides.body: On GPT-5.5 in direct chat, procedural tasks saw the largest jumps: SpreadsheetBench rose from 41.8 to 80.7, OfficeQA from 33.1 to 72.1, LiveMathematicianBench from 37.6 to 66.9. Averaged across benchmarks, SkillOpt lifted GPT-5.5's accuracy by +23.5 points in direct chat, +24.8 inside the Codex agentic loop, and +19.1 inside Claude Code.
bullets: SpreadsheetBench: 41.8 → 80.7 (GPT-5.5, direct chat, no-skill vs. SkillOpt-trained skill)
A spreadsheet skill trained entirely inside the Codex loop was dropped into Claude Code unchanged
That transferred skill drove a +59.7 point gain over Claude Code's own native baseline

slides.title: Where Text-Space Optimization Runs Out of Road
slides.body: SkillOpt trains the instructions, not the model — which is exactly its limit as well as its strength.
bullets: It needs a scorable validation signal for every task; skills for hard-to-grade, open-ended work have less to optimize against
A single bounded skill document can't fix a base model that's fundamentally incapable of a task
Training still costs optimizer-model calls and rollouts, even though deployment is free of overhead
Bounded add/delete/replace edits favor incremental improvement over rethinking a skill from scratch

slides.title: Takeaways
slides.body: SkillOpt reframes 'prompt engineering for agents' as a real optimization problem: score it, edit it, validate it, keep only what wins.
bullets: Skills become external, trainable state for a frozen agent — no weight updates, no extra inference cost
Validation-gated edits (not free-form self-revision) are what make the process reliable enough to beat 6 prior methods
Best-or-tied on 52/52 tested cells, with gains up to +24.8 points and skills that transfer across execution harnesses
The tradeoff: it needs a scorable task and rollouts to learn from — it isn't magic for ungraded, wide-open work

questions.prompt: What does SkillOpt actually optimize?
options: The agent model's weights via fine-tuning
A natural-language skill document, treated as the agent's external trainable state
The tokenizer vocabulary
A separate reward model used for RLHF
questions.explanation: SkillOpt keeps the agent frozen and instead edits a skill markdown file — treating that document as the thing to train, with zero added inference-time cost at deployment.

questions.prompt: How does SkillOpt decide whether to keep a proposed edit to the skill document?
options: It always accepts the optimizer model's suggested edit
It picks the edit with the most words changed
It keeps the edit only if it strictly improves a held-out validation score
It asks a human reviewer for every edit
questions.explanation: Validation-gated updates are central to SkillOpt: an edit ships only when it strictly beats the current held-out score, which is what keeps the optimization from drifting or thrashing.

questions.prompt: What happened when a skill trained inside the Codex harness was moved into Claude Code unchanged?
options: It failed completely and had to be retrained
It drove a +59.7 point gain over Claude Code's native baseline
Performance was identical to no skill at all
It only worked after converting it to a different file format
questions.explanation: The transferred spreadsheet skill produced a +59.7 point gain over Claude Code's own baseline with no further changes — evidence that a trained skill document can generalize across execution harnesses, not just across prompts.

posts.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Skip the Reward Model
posts.subtitle: One algebraic trick turns RLHF's three-stage pipeline into a single loss over preference pairs — no reward model, no PPO rollouts.
posts.summary: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) reformulates RLHF's reward-model-plus-PPO pipeline as a single supervised-style loss computed directly over the policy's log-probabilities on chosen vs. rejected responses. It's mathematically the same KL-constrained objective as RLHF, just reparameterized so the reward is implicit — trading online RL infrastructure for something that trains like ordinary fine-tuning, at the cost of being fully offline.

slides.title: Same Alignment Goal, No Reward Model Required
slides.body: DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023) showed that the standard RLHF objective has a closed-form solution linking the optimal policy to its reward function — invert that relationship, and you can train directly on preference pairs without ever fitting a separate reward model.
bullets: Reframes preference alignment as one loss over (chosen, rejected) response pairs
No reward model, no online sampling, no PPO — trains like standard supervised fine-tuning
Became the default recipe behind many open preference-tuned models

slides.title: Classic RLHF Is a Three-Stage Rube Goldberg Machine
slides.body: The original RLHF recipe chains three separate training runs, each with its own failure modes.
bullets: Stage 1: supervised fine-tune on demonstrations
Stage 2: train a separate reward model on human preference pairs
Stage 3: run PPO against that reward model — online sampling, a value network, and a KL penalty to stop reward hacking
Each extra stage adds its own hyperparameters and its own way to become unstable

slides.title: The Reward Model Has a Closed Form — So Skip Training It
slides.body: For the KL-constrained reward-maximization objective RLHF actually optimizes, the optimal policy can be written in closed form in terms of the reward. DPO inverts that equation to express the reward implicitly in terms of the policy itself, then plugs it into the standard Bradley-Terry preference model.
bullets: Loss is a binary cross-entropy over the log-probability ratio (policy vs. frozen reference) of chosen minus rejected
A temperature β controls how strongly the loss pushes on that ratio
No reward network, no value network, no online rollouts — just forward passes over static preference pairs

slides.title: What Training Actually Needs
slides.body: DPO's infrastructure footprint is close to ordinary supervised fine-tuning.
bullets: A static dataset of (prompt, chosen response, rejected response) triples — no scalar reward labels needed
A frozen reference model (usually the pre-DPO SFT checkpoint); its log-probs can be precomputed once and cached
β is the key hyperparameter: lower β lets the policy move further from the reference toward the preference signal, higher β keeps it conservative

slides.title: RLHF (PPO) vs. DPO

rows: No — reward stays implicit in the policy
No — trains on a static offline dataset
Sensitive to reward hacking, needs KL tuning

slides.title: A Worked Preference Pair
slides.body: Prompt: 'Explain recursion to a beginner.' Chosen: a short, concrete example (a function that counts down). Rejected: a jargon-heavy definition with no example.
bullets: DPO's gradient increases the chosen response's log-probability relative to the reference model
It simultaneously decreases the rejected response's log-probability relative to the reference
The update is weighted by how wrong the model's current implicit reward estimate is — bigger correction where the model is more confidently wrong

slides.title: When DPO Struggles
slides.body: Removing the reward model and PPO loop doesn't remove every failure mode.
bullets: Fully offline: quality is capped by the static preference dataset — it can't explore responses an online RL loop might discover
Sensitive to noisy or inconsistent preference labels, with no reward-model averaging to smooth outliers
If the policy drifts far from the reference during training, the original preference pairs may stop reflecting what the new policy would actually produce
Newer variants (IPO, KTO, ORPO) exist specifically to patch known DPO failure modes like margin overfitting

slides.title: Takeaways
slides.body: DPO is a reparameterization of RLHF's objective, not a different goal.
bullets: Same KL-constrained reward-maximization objective as RLHF, just solved so the reward stays implicit in the policy
Trains like supervised fine-tuning: no reward model, no online sampling, no value network
Far simpler infrastructure — but fully offline, so it's only as good as its static preference dataset
A starting point: later variants (IPO, KTO, ORPO) trade off different parts of the same idea

questions.prompt: What does DPO's loss function operate on directly?
options: A scalar reward model's output
The log-probability ratio between the trained policy and a frozen reference model, on chosen vs. rejected responses
Token-level cross-entropy against ground-truth text
A learned value function estimating future reward
questions.explanation: DPO reformulates preference learning as a classification loss over the implicit reward, computed from log-probability ratios between the policy and a frozen reference — no separate reward model is ever trained.

questions.prompt: Why does DPO keep a frozen reference model during training?
options: To generate new synthetic preference pairs
To act as a critic inside a PPO loop
To anchor the update so the policy doesn't drift arbitrarily far while fitting the preference data
Because the reward model needs a baseline to subtract
questions.explanation: The frozen reference (usually the SFT checkpoint) keeps the KL-constrained part of the objective intact — without it, nothing stops the policy from overfitting to preference pairs in a way that wrecks its general behavior.

questions.prompt: What is a key limitation of DPO compared to online RLHF with PPO?
options: It requires substantially more GPU memory than PPO
It cannot use human preference data at all
It's fully offline, so it can't explore responses outside its static preference dataset
It only works on models under 1B parameters
questions.explanation: Because DPO trains on a fixed, pre-collected set of preference pairs, it can't discover and evaluate new responses the way an online RL loop can — its ceiling is set by the dataset it started with.

references.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model (Rafailov et al., 2023)

posts.title: Looped World Models: One Block, Iterated, Instead of Stacked
posts.subtitle: A parameter-shared transformer block that loops on its own latent state hits up to 100x the parameter efficiency of stacking more layers.
posts.summary: Looped World Models (LoopWM) replace a deep stack of distinct transformer layers with a single shared block that iterates on its own latent state, using an adaptive early-exit rule to spend more iterations on hard transitions and fewer on easy ones. The result: comparable simulation depth at up to 100x fewer parameters, at the cost of extra sequential inference steps per prediction.

slides.title: The First Looped Architecture Built for World Simulation
slides.body: Posted to arXiv on June 16, 2026 by researchers at FaceMind Research Asia, 'Looped World Models' proposes iterative latent refinement as a new axis for scaling world models — orthogonal to adding parameters or training data.
bullets: arXiv:2606.18208 — 'Looped World Models'
Core idea: one shared transformer block, run in a loop, instead of many distinct stacked layers
Reported result: up to 100x parameter efficiency over conventional stacked world models

slides.title: Long-Horizon Simulation Wants Depth; Depth Is Expensive
slides.body: A world model has to predict how an environment's state evolves many steps ahead. Faithful long-horizon rollouts need deep computation per step, but stacking more distinct layers to get that depth means more parameters to store, more memory to move, and errors that compound across a longer chain of learned transformations.
bullets: Deeper stacked models = linearly more parameters, regardless of whether a given step actually needs that much computation
Every prediction step pays the same fixed depth, even trivially predictable transitions
Compounding error over long rollouts gets worse as more distinct, imperfectly-learned layers are chained

slides.title: Loop the Same Block Instead of Stacking New Ones
slides.body: LoopWM shares one transformer block across every iteration of refinement. Instead of layer 1, layer 2, layer 3 each holding separate weights, the same weights are applied repeatedly to progressively refine the latent state — so total parameter count stays constant no matter how many iterations are used.
bullets: A parameter-shared block iteratively refines the latent environment state, rather than passing it through distinct layers
A contractive state-retention mechanism keeps the repeated iteration numerically stable instead of diverging
Model capacity (loop depth) becomes decoupled from parameter count for the first time in this class of model

slides.title: Adaptive Early-Exit: Spend Iterations Where They're Needed
slides.body: Not every transition deserves the same compute. LoopWM adds an early-exit strategy that halts the loop once the latent state stabilizes, so simple, predictable transitions exit after one or two iterations while complex, uncertain ones keep looping.
bullets: The loop count is decided per-prediction-step, not fixed in advance
Predictable transitions (e.g. an object continuing in a straight line) exit early, saving compute
Harder, higher-uncertainty transitions (e.g. a collision or contact event) get more refinement iterations automatically

slides.title: Same Effective Depth, a Fraction of the Weights
slides.body: The headline efficiency claim is about parameters, not FLOPs: reusing one block across iterations means adding effective depth costs no new parameters, unlike a conventional stacked model where each added layer of depth is new weights to store and move.

rows: One more iteration, no new weights
Adaptive — fewer iterations on easy steps
Constant, up to 100x fewer for comparable depth

slides.title: Worked Example: A Robot Arm Reaching for an Object
slides.body: Picture a world model simulating a robot arm's next 50 states while reaching for a cup. For most of the reach, the arm's trajectory is smooth and predictable — LoopWM's early-exit rule lets those steps finish in one or two loop iterations.
bullets: Smooth, in-flight motion: early exit after minimal iterations, cheap to simulate
The moment of contact with the cup — where friction, grip, and possible slippage matter — is exactly where uncertainty spikes
The loop automatically spends more iterations refining the latent state around that contact event, without any manual tuning of 'how deep should this step be'

slides.title: A New Scaling Axis — Not a Free Lunch
slides.body: LoopWM's tradeoff is sequential: iterations happen one after another on the same block, so a harder prediction step takes more wall-clock time even though it costs no extra parameters. That makes it a strong fit when memory and storage are the binding constraint, less so when per-step latency is.
bullets: Best for: deployments where parameter count/memory is the bottleneck, not raw per-step latency
Not a drop-in win when hardware is already optimized for wide, parallel stacked inference
Establishes iterative latent depth as a scaling axis orthogonal to model size and data — a third lever alongside 'bigger' and 'more data'
Adaptive early-exit is the key trick that keeps average-case compute low despite the loop

questions.prompt: What does LoopWM share across iterations that a conventional stacked world model does not?
options: The training dataset
The transformer block's weights — the same block is applied repeatedly
The optimizer's learning rate schedule
The tokenizer vocabulary
questions.explanation: LoopWM iterates the same parameter-shared transformer block on the latent state, instead of stacking distinct layers with separate weights — that's why adding depth doesn't add parameters.

questions.prompt: What decides how many loop iterations LoopWM spends on a given prediction step?
options: A fixed number set identically for every step
The size of the input image
An adaptive early-exit rule that halts once the latent state stabilizes
A random number generator for regularization
questions.explanation: The adaptive early-exit mechanism lets easy, predictable transitions stop after few iterations while harder, more uncertain transitions keep refining — matching compute to difficulty automatically.

questions.prompt: What is the main tradeoff LoopWM makes to achieve its parameter efficiency?
options: It requires far more training data than stacked models
It cannot be used for long-horizon prediction
Harder prediction steps take more sequential wall-clock time, since iterations run one after another on the same block
It only works on text data, not simulated environments
questions.explanation: Because the loop iterations are sequential on a shared block, saving parameters comes at the cost of variable, step-dependent latency rather than the fixed, parallelizable cost of a stacked model.

posts.title: Consistent Hashing: Adding a Server Without Reshuffling Everything
posts.subtitle: Plain mod-N hashing remaps almost every key when your cluster resizes. A hash ring fixes that by moving only a sliver of the keys.
posts.summary: Consistent hashing places both servers and keys on a hash ring so that each key belongs to the next server clockwise. Adding or removing a node then remaps only about 1/N of the keys instead of nearly all of them, which is why it underpins distributed caches and databases like DynamoDB, Cassandra, and memcached.

slides.title: Why Adding One Server Shouldn't Break Your Whole Cache
slides.body: Consistent hashing is the technique that lets a distributed cache or database resize its cluster while relocating only a small fraction of its keys — instead of nearly all of them.
bullets: Used in DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra, Riak, and memcached client libraries
Solves the 'resizing invalidates everything' problem of naive hash-mod-N sharding
Core structure: a ring of hash values, not a flat array of buckets

slides.title: Mod-N Hashing Reshuffles Almost Everything
slides.body: The obvious way to shard is server = hash(key) % N. It's simple and balances load evenly — until N changes. Add or remove one server and N shifts, which changes the result of the modulo for nearly every key.
bullets: Going from 4 servers to 5 changes hash(key) % 4 to hash(key) % 5 for almost every key
In the worst case, (N-1)/N of all keys map to a different server — for N=5, that's 80% of your cache invalidated at once
Every one of those 'moved' keys is a cache miss or a data-location lookup failure right when the cluster is under the stress of a resize

slides.title: Put Servers and Keys on the Same Ring
slides.body: Consistent hashing hashes both server identifiers and keys into the same fixed space (say, 0 to 2^32-1) and imagines that space as a ring. Each key is owned by the first server whose hash value appears going clockwise from the key's own hash position.
bullets: Servers become points on the ring, not slots in an array — order and count no longer matter the same way
A key's owner is found by walking clockwise to the nearest server hash — no modulo by the current node count
Removing or adding a server only shifts ownership of the keys between it and its previous neighbor on the ring

slides.title: Virtual Nodes Fix the Ring's Lumpy Load Problem
slides.body: With only one ring point per physical server, load balance depends entirely on luck — a few servers can end up owning much larger arcs of the ring than others. Virtual nodes fix this by giving each physical server many points (often 100-300) scattered around the ring.
bullets: Each physical server is represented by many virtual points instead of one
More virtual points per server smooths out the ring, so each server ends up owning a similar total arc length
This is what makes systems like Cassandra and DynamoDB tune a 'vnodes per node' setting rather than relying on raw consistent hashing

slides.title: Mod-N vs. Consistent Hashing vs. Consistent Hashing + Vnodes

rows: Even, but only while N is fixed
Uneven — arcs vary a lot by luck
Even, smooths out with more vnodes

slides.title: Worked Example: Growing a 4-Node Ring to 5 Nodes
slides.body: Four servers (A, B, C, D) sit at four points on the ring, each owning the arc counter-clockwise back to the previous server. You add server E between C and D on the ring.
bullets: Only the keys that used to belong to D, but now fall between C and E, move to E
A, B, and C's keys are completely untouched by the resize
Roughly 1/5 of the total keyspace moves — not the 80% that mod-N hashing would have shuffled for the same 4-to-5 resize

slides.title: When It Helps, and What It Doesn't Solve
slides.body: Consistent hashing solves key-to-node placement under resizing. It does not, by itself, handle replication, node failure detection, or heterogeneous server capacity — those need to be layered on top.
bullets: Without virtual nodes, load can still be quite uneven — always tune vnode count, don't use bare consistent hashing
Replication (e.g. Dynamo-style 'store on the next N successors around the ring') is a separate layer built on top of the ring
Weighted vnode counts handle servers with different capacity, but that weighting has to be configured, not automatic
Best for: caches and stores that resize often and can't afford to invalidate most of their keys on every change

questions.prompt: With plain hash(key) % N sharding, roughly what fraction of keys can be remapped when you add one more server?
options: None — mod-N hashing is resize-safe
Up to (N-1)/N of all keys
Exactly 1/N of all keys
Only the keys on the busiest server
questions.explanation: Changing N changes the modulo result for nearly every key, so up to (N-1)/N of all keys can end up on a different server — for N=5 that's about 80%.

questions.prompt: What problem do virtual nodes solve in a consistent hashing ring?
options: They encrypt keys for security
They replace the need for a hash function entirely
They smooth out uneven load by giving each physical server many points on the ring instead of one
They eliminate the need for replication
questions.explanation: With one ring point per server, arc lengths (and thus load) vary a lot by chance. Spreading each server across many virtual points averages this out.

questions.prompt: When a node is added to a consistent hashing ring, which keys actually move?
options: All keys in the cluster
Only the keys between the new node and its predecessor on the ring
Only the keys on the node with the least load
No keys move — the ring is rebuilt from scratch
questions.explanation: The new node only takes ownership of the arc between itself and the previous server going clockwise — every other server's keys are untouched.

posts.title: Program-as-Weights: Compiling English Into a Neural Function
posts.subtitle: A 4B compiler turns a natural-language spec into a LoRA adapter that a frozen 0.6B model runs offline, at 1/50th the memory of prompting a 32B model.
posts.summary: Program-as-Weights (PAW) treats a natural-language function description as source code: a compiler emits a small LoRA adapter that a frozen, tiny interpreter model executes locally, matching a 32B model's output quality at a fraction of the memory and with no API call. The tradeoff is a fixed capability ceiling set by the interpreter's size.

slides.title: A Compiler That Turns English Into a Neural Function
slides.body: Program-as-Weights (PAW), from a paper posted to arXiv on July 2, 2026, was the #1 trending paper on Hugging Face's Daily Papers with 113 upvotes.
bullets: arXiv:2607.02512 — 'Program-as-Weights: A Programming Paradigm for Fuzzy Functions'
Core idea: compile a fuzzy natural-language spec into a portable neural artifact
Trained compiler: 4B params, on FuzzyBench (10M examples, 800+ task categories)

slides.title: Calling an LLM API for Every Small Judgment Call Is Wasteful
slides.body: Real software is full of 'fuzzy functions' — is this ticket urgent, does this address look valid, does this comment need moderation — that are too vague for regex but too small to justify a full 32B-parameter model call every time.
bullets: Each call pays network round-trip latency plus full model inference cost
Outputs can drift as the hosted model is silently updated
You can't cache, version, or run these functions offline

slides.title: The Spec Compiles Into a LoRA Adapter, Not a Prompt
slides.body: PAW's compiler is itself a neural network with two stages: a pseudo-compiler turns the English spec into a clean pseudo-program, then a LoRA compiler reads spec and pseudo-program to emit LoRA weights for a frozen interpreter.
bullets: Output is a single file: a LoRA adapter, cacheable and version-controllable like a library
The interpreter itself (a 0.6B Qwen3 model) never changes — only the adapter does
No fine-tuning loop per function: compilation is a forward pass through the compiler

slides.title: Three Moving Parts: Spec, Compiler, Frozen Interpreter
bullets: Developer writes the function in plain English, once
4B compiler (pseudo-compiler + LoRA compiler) emits a small adapter file
0.6B frozen interpreter loads the adapter at call time and executes it locally
Same interpreter reused across every compiled function — only the adapter swaps

slides.title: 0.6B Interpreter Matches a 32B Model on Its Own Task
slides.body: The headline result: running a compiled PAW program on the 0.6B interpreter matches directly prompting Qwen3-32B on the same fuzzy function.

slides.title: Worked Example: A Support-Ticket Urgency Classifier
slides.body: A developer writes: 'Given a support ticket's subject and body, return urgent, normal, or low based on churn risk, outage language, and sentiment.' PAW compiles that sentence into an adapter.
bullets: The adapter file is committed to the repo like any other config asset
At runtime, the 0.6B interpreter loads it and classifies tickets with no network call
FuzzyBench's 800+ categories also cover format conversion, parsing, and agentic tool-argument extraction — the same recipe applies to those

slides.title: When This Pattern Wins — and Its Ceiling
slides.body: PAW is a strong fit for narrow, well-specified 'fuzzy function' calls you make thousands of times — not a replacement for open-ended chat or tasks needing broad world knowledge.
bullets: Best for: repeated, narrow judgment calls where you'd otherwise hammer a hosted API
Not for: open-ended reasoning or tasks beyond what a 0.6B interpreter can represent
Each new spec still needs a (cheap) compile pass — it's not zero-cost to add a function
Net win: offline execution, ~50x less memory, and an adapter you can cache and diff like code

questions.prompt: What does the PAW compiler actually emit for a given natural-language function spec?
options: A LoRA adapter for a frozen interpreter model
A fully fine-tuned copy of the interpreter
A Python function generated by the LLM
A cached prompt template
questions.explanation: PAW's two-stage compiler (pseudo-compiler + LoRA compiler) turns the spec into a small LoRA adapter — a portable file, not a new model or a prompt.

questions.prompt: How big is the frozen interpreter that matched Qwen3-32B's performance on compiled fuzzy functions?
options: 7B
questions.explanation: A 0.6B Qwen3 interpreter running the compiled adapter matched direct-prompting a 32B model, at roughly 1/50th the inference memory.

questions.prompt: What is FuzzyBench, in this paper?
options: A 10M-example dataset spanning 800+ fuzzy-function task categories, used to train the compiler
A latency benchmark for GPU inference servers
A held-out test set of exact-match algorithmic problems
A leaderboard for chatbot preference ranking
questions.explanation: FuzzyBench is the training data for the neural compiler — 10 million examples across classification, format conversion, parsing, fuzzy matching, and agentic tool use.

references.title: Program-as-Weights: A Programming Paradigm for Fuzzy Functions (arXiv:2607.02512)
Paper page — Hugging Face Daily Papers

posts.title: GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, Luna: OpenAI Ships Three Tiers, One for Cyber Ops
posts.subtitle: A flagship built for vulnerability research, a mid-tier at half the cost, and an economy model — previewed to ~20 vetted partners under US government coordination.
posts.summary: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6 as three models — Sol, Terra, and Luna — with Sol positioned as its most capable cybersecurity agent yet, matching Anthropic's Mythos Preview on ExploitBench at a third of the output tokens. Access is limited to roughly 20 vetted partners, with the US government involved in that gating given the dual-use offensive-security capability.

slides.title: One Model Family, Three Very Different Jobs
slides.body: OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6 on June 26, 2026, splitting it into Sol (flagship), Terra (balanced), and Luna (fast/cheap) rather than shipping a single model.
bullets: Sol: OpenAI's most capable model yet for cybersecurity work
Terra: GPT-5.5-competitive performance at roughly half the cost
Luna: the fastest and cheapest tier of the three

slides.title: Why Split Into Tiers — and Why Gate Access at All
slides.body: Frontier labs increasingly ship tiered families instead of one model, trading capability for cost/latency by use case. Sol adds a second wrinkle: real offensive-cyber capability significant enough that OpenAI coordinated the rollout with the US government.
bullets: Not every workload needs (or can afford) flagship-tier reasoning
Sol's vulnerability-research ability is dual-use: useful for defenders, dangerous in the wrong hands
Initial access: ~20 trusted partners only, broader release promised 'in the coming weeks'

slides.title: Sol Is Built and Hardened Specifically for Security Work
slides.body: Sol shifts the performance-efficiency frontier for long-horizon security tasks — vulnerability research and exploitation — and ships with what OpenAI calls its most robust safety stack to date.
bullets: Weeks of red-teaming aimed specifically at cyber-misuse and jailbreak pressure
Strengthened protections for higher-risk activity and repeated-misuse patterns
A HackerOne-style posture: built assuming determined adversarial probing

slides.title: Sol vs. Claude Mythos on the Benchmarks That Matter Here
slides.body: On ExploitBench, Sol matches Anthropic's Mythos Preview using about a third of the output tokens — though the full Mythos 5 model still leads outright.

slides.title: Ultra Mode: One Task, a Team of Subagents
slides.body: Sol's 'Ultra' mode spins up subagents to split a long-horizon task — for example, a sandboxed penetration test where one subagent maps the attack surface while another drafts and tests exploit code — pushing Terminal-Bench 2.1 from 88.8% to 91.9%.
bullets: Subagent coordination is what buys the extra ~3 points over standard mode
Terra at half Sol's output price targets everyday agentic coding, not offensive security
Luna is the volume tier: cheapest per token, no cyber-specialization

slides.title: The Catch: Gated Access and an Unfinished Rollout
slides.body: OpenAI itself has called the current government-coordinated access model unsustainable long-term — this is a preview, not a general-availability launch.
bullets: Only ~20 vetted partners have Sol access as of the preview
'Coming weeks' for ChatGPT/Codex/API availability is not a firm date
Sol's premium pricing ($5/$30 per M tokens) reflects its narrower, specialized use case

slides.title: The Pattern to Watch
slides.body: GPT-5.6 confirms a broader 2026 trend: frontier labs no longer ship one model, they ship a tiered family, and the most capable tier increasingly comes with capability-specific safety gating rather than open release.
bullets: Efficiency claim: Sol ≈ Mythos Preview quality at ~1/3 the output tokens on ExploitBench
Sol Ultra sets a new agentic-coding mark: 91.9% on Terminal-Bench 2.1
Watch for: whether the ~20-partner gate widens on OpenAI's promised timeline

questions.prompt: What sets GPT-5.6 Sol apart from Terra and Luna?
options: It's the flagship, specialized for long-horizon cybersecurity work like vulnerability research
It's the cheapest of the three tiers
It's the only one available via the public API today
It has no safety mitigations, unlike Terra and Luna
questions.explanation: Sol is positioned as OpenAI's most capable model yet for cybersecurity, shifting the performance-efficiency frontier for vulnerability research and exploitation tasks.

questions.prompt: On ExploitBench, how did Sol's efficiency compare to Anthropic's Mythos Preview?
options: Sol matched its performance using about a third of the output tokens
Sol used 3x more output tokens for the same performance
Sol outperformed it by a wide margin on every metric
They were not compared on ExploitBench
questions.explanation: Sol matched Mythos Preview's ExploitBench performance while consuming roughly one-third of the output tokens, though full Mythos 5 still led outright.

questions.prompt: Why is initial access to GPT-5.6 Sol limited to roughly 20 partners?
options: OpenAI coordinated the rollout with the US government given Sol's dual-use offensive-cyber capability
OpenAI ran out of GPU capacity to serve more users
Terra and Luna are being prioritized instead
It's a pricing experiment unrelated to safety
questions.explanation: Sol's vulnerability-research and exploitation capability is significant enough that the preview's partner access was coordinated with the US government at its request.

posts.title: Prompt Caching: Pay Full Price Once, Reuse the Context for Cheap
posts.subtitle: How Anthropic and OpenAI let repeated prompt prefixes skip recomputation — and the one-character mistake that silently kills the discount.
posts.summary: Prompt caching stores the processed attention state of a repeated prompt prefix so later calls skip recomputing it — cutting cost and latency for agents and RAG pipelines that resend the same system prompt, tool schema, or document turn after turn. It only pays off when the prefix is byte-identical and reused within the cache's TTL.

slides.title: The Context You Resend Every Turn Doesn't Have to Be Recomputed
slides.body: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and AWS Bedrock all now offer prompt caching — reusing a prior request's processed prompt instead of recomputing it from scratch.
bullets: Same idea, different mechanics per provider
Biggest win for agents and RAG: large static context, repeated many times per session

slides.title: Every Call Reprocesses the Whole Prompt From Scratch
slides.body: A coding agent might resend a 20,000-token system prompt and tool schema on every one of 50 turns in a session — 19,990 of those tokens are identical to the previous call, yet get fully recomputed anyway.
bullets: You pay full input-token price for content the model has already seen
Recomputing a long static prefix adds real latency before the model can even start responding
This compounds fast for multi-turn agents and RAG pipelines with big injected context

slides.title: Cache the Attention State of the Prefix, Not Just the Text
slides.body: When a request's prompt prefix exactly matches a recently-cached one, the provider reuses the already-computed key/value attention state for that prefix and only runs fresh compute on the new tokens after it.
bullets: Anthropic: explicit — you mark a cache_control breakpoint in the request
OpenAI: automatic — no code change, applies to prompts over 1,024 tokens
Both require the cached prefix to match exactly, token for token

slides.title: Anthropic vs. OpenAI: The Actual Numbers
Worked Example: Break-Even on a 5-Minute Cache
slides.body: You send a 20k-token system prompt + tool schema with Anthropic's 5-minute cache. The first call writes the cache at 1.25x price. Every call after that, within 5 minutes, reads it at 0.10x price.
bullets: Write once at 1.25x — a 25% premium over the normal price for that prefix
First cache read already discounts to 0.10x, recovering the 25% premium and then some
Net effect: break-even after just 1 cache hit; every hit after that is nearly pure savings

slides.title: The Silent Failure Mode: One Changed Character Breaks the Cache
slides.body: Caching only matches an exact prefix. A live timestamp, a random request ID, or even reordering a tool list inside the 'cached' portion invalidates the whole thing — and you're back to paying full price with no error or warning.
bullets: Put static content (system prompt, tool defs, long documents) first
Put dynamic content (user message, timestamps, session IDs) after the cache breakpoint
A cache write premium with no follow-up reads makes a one-off call pricier, not cheaper

slides.title: When to Reach for It
slides.body: Prompt caching is a clear win exactly when you'd expect: large, static, repeated context, called often enough within the TTL window to amortize the write cost.
bullets: Use it for: agent system prompts, tool schemas, long RAG documents reused across turns
Skip it for: one-shot calls, or prompts where the 'static' part keeps changing anyway
Structure prompts static-first, dynamic-last so the cache boundary actually lands where you want it

questions.prompt: What does a prompt cache actually store between requests?
options: The processed key/value attention state for a matching prompt prefix
A compressed copy of the model's weights
The user's previous chat history in a database
A summary of the conversation generated by a smaller model
questions.explanation: Caching reuses the already-computed attention state for an identical prompt prefix, so the model doesn't recompute it on the next call.

questions.prompt: With Anthropic's 5-minute TTL cache (1.25x write, 0.10x read), how many cache reads does it take to break even on the write premium?
options: It never breaks even
questions.explanation: A single 0.10x-priced read already recovers more than the 0.25x premium paid on the write, so you break even after just one hit.

questions.prompt: Why would putting a live timestamp at the start of a system prompt hurt prompt caching?
options: Because caching requires an exact prefix match, so a changing value there invalidates the cache on every call
Because timestamps are too short to be cached
Because it forces the request to use a different, more expensive model
Timestamps don't affect caching at all
questions.explanation: Cache matching is exact-prefix based — any part of the prefix that changes between calls, including a timestamp, breaks the match and forces full recomputation.

Prompt Caching in the API — OpenAI

posts.title: AutoMem: When Agent Memory Becomes a Trainable Skill
posts.subtitle: A meta-LLM rewrites the scaffold, then trains a memory specialist — pushing a 32B open model past its own baseline on long-horizon games.
posts.summary: AutoMem (arXiv:2607.01224) treats memory as a trainable cognitive skill instead of a fixed architecture: one meta-LLM loop rewrites the agent's memory scaffold, another trains a dedicated memory specialist via LoRA. On Crafter, MiniHack, and NetHack, it lifts a 32B open-weight model roughly 2x-4x in progression rate over the un-tuned baseline.

slides.body: AutoMem, from a Stanford research team (Shengguang Wu, Hao Zhu, Yuhui Zhang, Xiaohan Wang, Serena Yeung-Levy), makes memory management a first-class, learnable behavior for LLM agents.
slides.title: Memory Is a Skill You Train, Not a Buffer You Build

bullets: Hand-written scaffolds don't adapt: what's worth remembering in Crafter isn't what's worth remembering in NetHack
Fixed-format memory means the agent can't learn better retrieval habits from its own mistakes
Base open-weight models (e.g. Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct) lag frontier systems mostly on long-horizon tasks, not short ones
slides.body: A game like NetHack can run for thousands of steps. Cramming the full trajectory into context is impossible, so most agents bolt on a hand-designed memory scaffold — a fixed template for what to log and when to check it.
slides.title: Long-Horizon Agents Drown in Their Own History

bullets: Loop 1 (scaffold evolution): a meta-LLM reads full episode traces, diagnoses memory-use failures, and rewrites the agent's scaffold — keeping a revision only if it improves progression on fixed eval seeds
Loop 2 (proficiency training): a training engine mines the base model's own traces for good memory decisions, then trains a dedicated memory specialist with LoRA
Task-action policy is left untouched — every gain comes purely from better memory habits
slides.body: AutoMem promotes LOG and PLAN — writing to and reading from a memory directory — to first-class actions, on equal footing with in-game moves. Two optimization loops then improve how that's done.
slides.title: Two Nested Loops, Two Different Jobs

bullets: LOG step after each encounter appends a short structured note to a memory file (what happened, what worked)
PLAN step retrieves relevant notes before a risky action and folds them into the next decision
The scaffold loop learns which categories of notes actually change outcomes and prunes the rest
slides.body: Before a risky descent, the PLAN routine forces the agent to consult its memory files — e.g. 'last time I fought a jackal pack at low HP, I died' — before acting, rather than reacting blind.
slides.title: Worked Example: Surviving Deeper Into NetHack

bullets: Across all three environments, memory optimization alone delivered roughly a 2x-4x gain in progression rate over the base agent
slides.body: Numbers below are the agent's end-state after optimizing memory alone — no change to the underlying policy weights beyond the memory specialist.

rows: NetHack's full game is brutally deep — even small gains here are hard-won

slides.title: Final-State Progression, Base Model Only

bullets: Loop 2 training has real cost: mining traces and fine-tuning a memory specialist isn't free, and needs enough failed/successful episodes to learn from
It optimizes memory, not the underlying policy — a fundamentally weak base model still hits a ceiling
Scaffold rewrites are only kept if they improve fixed eval seeds, which can overfit to those specific seeds rather than the task distribution generally
slides.body: AutoMem is validated on procedurally generated games, not on real-world tool-use agents (browsers, APIs, codebases) — the domains most production agents actually run in.
slides.title: Where This Doesn't (Yet) Reach

bullets: Treating memory read/write as trainable actions — not fixed plumbing — let a 32B open model close ground on frontier systems using only smarter memory habits
The two-loop split (scaffold structure vs. retrieval proficiency) separates 'what to remember' from 'how to use it,' and each can improve independently
Biggest open question: does this transfer from game benchmarks to messy real-world agent tasks like coding or research assistants?
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.explanation: Loop 1 is a meta-LLM that reads full episode traces, diagnoses where memory use failed, and rewrites the scaffold — keeping changes only if they improve progression on fixed eval seeds.
questions.prompt: What does AutoMem's Loop 1 actually optimize?
options: The base model's weights via full fine-tuning
The agent's memory scaffold, by rewriting it based on diagnosed failures in episode traces
The game environment's difficulty
The tokenizer used for memory files

questions.explanation: NetHack is famous for its depth and difficulty — reaching dungeon level 2 at XP level 5 through better memory use alone, without touching the base policy, is a meaningful signal even though the raw percentage looks small.
questions.prompt: Why is NetHack's 2.91% progression result still notable?
options: Because NetHack is a short, easy game
Because the score is the highest of the three environments
Because NetHack is an extremely deep, unforgiving game where even small dungeon-level gains from memory alone are hard to achieve
Because 2.91% represents a full playthrough

questions.explanation: The reported gains are on game-like long-horizon benchmarks. Whether the same memory-as-a-skill approach transfers to real agentic work — coding, browsing, research — is still an open question.
questions.prompt: What's the main limitation of AutoMem's current evaluation?
options: It only works on models larger than 100B parameters
It has been validated on procedurally generated games (Crafter, MiniHack, NetHack), not real-world tool-use agents
It requires no training at all
It only improves short-horizon tasks

posts.title: DeepSeek Open-Sources Its Speculative Decoding Playbook
posts.subtitle: DeepSpec ships full training code for three draft-model algorithms — not just weights — so any team can build its own DSpark.
posts.summary: DeepSeek open-sourced DeepSpec, a full-stack MIT-licensed codebase for training and evaluating speculative-decoding draft models — bundling three algorithms (DSpark, DFlash, Eagle3) with pretrained checkpoints for Qwen3 (4B/8B/14B) and Gemma-4-12B-it. Unlike a weights-only drop, it hands infra teams the entire data-prep-to-eval pipeline used to build DSpark itself.

slides.body: DeepSeek didn't just release faster inference — it released the full pipeline used to build the draft models that make it faster.
slides.title: The Training Code Behind DSpark Is Now Public

bullets: A mismatched draft model has a low acceptance rate, so speculation buys little speedup
Until now, most teams could only use whatever pretrained draft checkpoints a lab chose to publish
DeepSeek's own DSpark reportedly makes its V4-Flash and V4-Pro models generate substantially faster in production, per DeepSeek's benchmarks — but only DeepSeek could reproduce how it was built
slides.body: Speculative decoding speeds up generation by having a small draft model guess several tokens ahead, which the big model then verifies in one pass. But a draft model tuned on someone else's prompt mix and hardware often guesses badly on yours.
slides.title: Speculative Decoding Only Helps If the Draft Model Fits

bullets: Three algorithms included: DSpark (DeepSeek's own), DFlash, and Eagle3
Pretrained checkpoints ship for Qwen3 (4B, 8B, 14B) and Google's Gemma-4-12B-it — not just DeepSeek's own models
Released under the MIT license, with attributions to SpecForge (Apache-2.0) and DFlash (MIT) for adapted code
slides.body: The new repository, deepseek-ai/DeepSpec, bundles three draft-model algorithms and lets teams train their own instead of depending on published checkpoints.
slides.title: DeepSpec: The Whole Stack, MIT-Licensed

bullets: 1. Data preparation — downloads prompts, regenerates target-model responses, and builds a cached-output dataset
2. Training — fits a draft model against those cached target outputs on a single 8-GPU node by default
3. Evaluation — measures speculative-decoding acceptance across nine benchmarks: GSM8K, MATH500, AIME25, HumanEval, MBPP, LiveCodeBench, MT-Bench, Alpaca, and Arena-Hard-v2
slides.title: A Three-Stage Pipeline, Not Just a Checkpoint

bullets: Per press coverage of DeepSeek's own benchmarks, DSpark reports roughly 60-85% faster generation for V4-Flash and 57-78% for V4-Pro versus DeepSeek's prior single-token-per-step baseline

rows: Nothing — accept whatever acceptance rate you get on your traffic
Draft model tuned to your own prompt distribution and hardware
A full 8-GPU training run; target-output cache can reach ~38TB

slides.title: What You're Actually Signing Up For

bullets: The default training configuration assumes a single node with 8 GPUs — this isn't a laptop-friendly fine-tune
The cached target-model outputs used for training can balloon to roughly 38TB of storage
Only two model families ship pretrained checkpoints (Qwen3, Gemma-4-12B-it) — anything else means building your own data pipeline first
slides.title: Before You Adopt It

bullets: DeepSeek open-sourced the training pipeline behind DSpark, not just inference weights — a meaningfully more useful release for infra teams
Three algorithms (DSpark, DFlash, Eagle3) ship in one repo, so teams can compare approaches rather than committing to one
The real cost isn't the code — it's the 8-GPU training run and tens of terabytes of cached target outputs needed to train a draft model well
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.explanation: Most speculative-decoding releases hand you a fixed draft checkpoint. DeepSpec instead ships the data-prep, training, and evaluation code so teams can train a draft model suited to their own traffic.
questions.prompt: What makes DeepSeek's DeepSpec release different from a typical model drop?
options: It only ships inference-optimized weights
It's closed-source but free to use commercially
It open-sources the full training pipeline for draft models, not just pretrained checkpoints
It replaces speculative decoding entirely with a new decoding method

questions.explanation: Speculative decoding's payoff depends on the draft model's acceptance rate — how often its guessed tokens match what the big model would have generated. A mismatched draft model guesses worse, so speedup shrinks.
questions.prompt: Why might a published draft-model checkpoint speed up generation less than expected?
options: Draft models never help with speculative decoding
If the draft model wasn't tuned on your prompt distribution and hardware, its guesses are accepted less often, reducing the speedup
Speculative decoding only works on TPUs
The target model becomes slower when paired with any draft model

questions.explanation: The data-preparation stage caches the target model's outputs for training the draft model, and that cache can grow to tens of terabytes — a real infrastructure cost beyond just GPU time.
questions.prompt: What's a real practical cost of training a custom draft model with DeepSpec?
options: It requires no GPUs at all
The cached target-model output dataset used for training can reach roughly 38TB
It only works with closed-source models
It takes less than a minute to train
