posts.title: Rotary Positional Embeddings: How LLMs Know Word Order
posts.subtitle: RoPE encodes position as a rotation, not an addition — and that's why it extrapolates
posts.summary: Attention has no built-in sense of word order — every token looks the same regardless of position unless you tell it otherwise. Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) inject position by rotating query and key vectors, so relative distance falls naturally out of the dot product. It's why most modern open LLMs (LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen) extrapolate to longer contexts far better than older absolute-position schemes.

slides.title: Attention Is Order-Blind by Default
slides.body: Self-attention computes a weighted sum over all tokens using dot products. Shuffle the input tokens and, without positional information, you'd get the exact same output — a transformer literally cannot tell 'dog bites man' from 'man bites dog'.

slides.title: Early Fixes Didn't Generalize
slides.body: The original Transformer added fixed sinusoidal position vectors to token embeddings. Later models learned absolute position embeddings from scratch instead.
bullets: Absolute embeddings tie a token's representation to its exact index — position 500 has a totally different vector than position 5,000
A model trained on 2k-token sequences has never seen a 'position 5,000' embedding at all, so it can't extrapolate past training length
What actually matters for meaning is usually relative distance between tokens ('3 words apart'), not their absolute index — absolute schemes don't encode that directly

slides.title: RoPE's Idea: Encode Position as a Rotation
slides.body: RoPE (Su et al., 2021) doesn't add a position vector to the embedding. It rotates the query and key vectors by an angle proportional to their position, in pairs of dimensions, before the dot product is taken.
bullets: Each pair of dimensions in Q and K gets rotated by θ·position, where θ varies by dimension pair (like the frequencies in sinusoidal encoding)
The rotation is applied at attention time, not baked into the token embedding itself
Crucially, rotating both Q and K means their dot product depends only on the relative rotation — i.e., the distance between the two positions — not on either absolute position

slides.title: Why the Rotation Trick Actually Works
slides.body: Rotation matrices compose by addition of angles: rotating by θ·m then θ·n is equivalent to a rotation by θ·(m-n) in the dot product. That algebraic property is the whole mechanism.
bullets: query at position m, key at position n → their dot product after rotation is a function of (m − n) alone
This gives relative position 'for free' out of an operation that only ever sees absolute positions m and n
No extra parameters are learned — the rotation angles are fixed by a formula, same family as the original sinusoidal scheme

slides.title: Absolute vs. Learned-Relative vs. RoPE

rows: Poorly — unseen indices have no embedding
Somewhat, but attention still position-sensitive in absolute terms
Reasonably, and extendable further with scaling tricks

slides.title: Worked Example: A 4,000-Token Prompt on a 2k-Trained Model
slides.body: Take a model pretrained with a 2k-token context using RoPE. Feed it a 4,000-token prompt.
bullets: With learned absolute embeddings, tokens past position 2,048 simply have no trained embedding — quality degrades sharply or the model errors out

With RoPE, the model has still only 'seen' relative distances up to ~2,048 during training, so accuracy still degrades past that — but gracefully, because the mechanism generalizes the same rotation formula to unseen distances
This gap is exactly why techniques like Position Interpolation and YaRN exist: they rescale RoPE's rotation frequencies so a model trained at 2k can be adapted to 32k+ context with modest fine-tuning

slides.title: Trade-offs and Pitfalls
slides.body: RoPE isn't free of downsides, and swapping it in isn't always the right call.
bullets: Raw extrapolation still degrades beyond trained context length — RoPE helps, but doesn't solve long-context for free; scaling methods (YaRN, NTK-aware scaling) are usually still needed
The rotation is applied per-attention-head at every layer, which is a bit more compute than a one-time embedding addition (though the cost is small in practice)

For short, fixed-length sequences (e.g., a classifier with a hard 128-token cap), simpler absolute or learned embeddings can be perfectly fine — RoPE's benefit is specifically about relative structure and length generalization

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Self-attention has no inherent sense of order — position must be injected explicitly, and how you do it shapes how well the model generalizes
RoPE rotates query/key vectors by an angle proportional to position, so their dot product depends only on relative distance
That relative-distance property is why LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, and most modern open LLMs extrapolate to longer contexts better than absolute-position predecessors
RoPE alone doesn't solve long-context — pair it with scaling techniques like YaRN when you need to push well past the trained length

questions.prompt: Why does raw self-attention need an explicit positional encoding scheme at all?
options: Because attention layers are too slow without it
Because attention's dot-product weighting is order-blind — shuffling tokens gives the same output without positional info
Because GPUs can't process sequences in parallel otherwise
Because the softmax function requires sorted input
questions.explanation: Self-attention computes weighted sums over all tokens via dot products; with no positional signal, 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' would look identical to the mechanism.

questions.prompt: What does RoPE actually do to encode position?
options: It adds a learned vector to each token embedding
It appends a position index as an extra token
It rotates the query and key vectors by an angle proportional to position before the dot product
It sorts tokens by position before attention
questions.explanation: RoPE rotates Q and K in pairs of dimensions by θ·position. Because rotation angles compose by subtraction, the resulting dot product depends only on relative distance (m − n).

questions.prompt: Why do RoPE-based models generally extrapolate to longer contexts better than models with learned absolute position embeddings?
options: RoPE models use more parameters overall
Learned absolute embeddings have no representation at all for indices past the trained length; RoPE's rotation formula still generalizes to unseen relative distances
RoPE disables attention for tokens past the trained length
There's no real difference — both extrapolate equally well
questions.explanation: An absolute embedding for position 5,000 simply doesn't exist if training only went to 2,048 — there's nothing to look up. RoPE's rotation is a formula, not a lookup table, so it degrades more gracefully (though scaling tricks like YaRN are still typically needed for large jumps).

references.title: RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding (Su et al., 2021)

posts.title: Libra: Fixing the GPU Waste in Agent Training
posts.subtitle: A scheduler that stops one slow tool call from stalling 48 GPUs
posts.summary: Libra is a resource-management system for agentic RL post-training that jointly schedules GPUs across the rollout and training stages. By replacing fragile length-based scheduling with a causality-driven queue and letting GPUs move fluidly between stages, it reports up to 3.0x higher throughput and 2.5x faster reward convergence on a 48-GPU cluster.

slides.body: Reinforcement learning has become the standard way to turn a base LLM into a capable agent. A June 2026 paper argues the infrastructure underneath that process is badly underutilized — and shows a fix.
slides.title: Training an Agent Wastes Most of Its GPUs

slides.body: In agentic RL, the 'rollout' stage has the model call real tools — search, code execution, browsers — to generate training trajectories. Tool calls don't take a fixed amount of time.
bullets: Rollout workloads are long-tailed: a small fraction of trajectories dominates total rollout time
Sequence lengths drift continuously as the policy improves, so static length predictions go stale
Rollout and training stages have different compute, memory, and sequence-length needs, but share the same fixed GPU pool
slides.title: One Slow Trajectory Stalls the Whole Batch

slides.body: Most agentic RL setups statically partition GPUs between rollout and training. That split can't adapt when rollout suddenly needs more capacity because of a few slow trajectories, or when training temporarily idles waiting on rollout to finish.
bullets: Cross-stage imbalance in compute pattern and memory footprint
Fixed partitioning can't react to workload drift within a single training run
The bottleneck isn't the model — it's the scheduler underneath it
slides.title: Two Stages, One Rigid GPU Split

slides.body: Libra pairs a global resource planner with a new scheduling policy built around cause, not just predicted length.
bullets: Global resource planner jointly optimizes GPU allocation across rollout and training clusters
An elastic hybrid pool lets workers move between stages without blocking the run
A causality-driven multilevel feedback queue (C-MLFQ) routes trajectories to fast/slow buckets using signals from actual tool-return outcomes
That replaces brittle length-prediction heuristics, which break down as the policy — and its outputs — keep changing
slides.title: Libra's Two Mechanisms

rows: Time to reach a target reward

slides.title: The Payoff, on 48 A800 GPUs

slides.body: Picture a batch of 256 rollout trajectories where a coding agent triggers a full test suite on 10 of them. Under static, length-based scheduling, the batch waits on those 10 slow test runs before training can start.
bullets: C-MLFQ detects the causal pattern — 'tool call returned late' — and demotes those trajectories to a slow-lane bucket
Fast trajectories keep flowing into training instead of queuing behind slow ones
The elastic pool can temporarily hand idle rollout GPUs to the training stage while the slow lane catches up
slides.title: Why a Coding Agent Benefits Most

bullets: Agentic RL's real bottleneck is often GPU scheduling, not model architecture
Static rollout/training GPU splits can't react to workload drift as the policy evolves
Libra routes work by causal signals (why a trajectory is slow) instead of fragile length predictions
Reported gains: up to 3.0x throughput and 2.5x faster convergence on 48 A800 GPUs
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.prompt: What specific problem does Libra target in agentic RL post-training?
options: Poor tokenizer efficiency
GPU scheduling imbalance between the rollout and training stages
A lack of labeled training data
Slow gradient descent convergence in supervised fine-tuning
questions.explanation: Libra is a resource-management system: it targets the mismatch in compute, memory, and timing needs between the rollout stage (tool-calling trajectory generation) and the training stage, which share a fixed GPU pool in most setups.

questions.prompt: Why does Libra avoid scheduling trajectories by predicted sequence length?
options: Sequence length is impossible to measure
Length predictions go stale because sequence-length distribution drifts as the policy evolves during training
Length-based scheduling is illegal under most cluster policies
GPUs cannot process variable-length sequences
questions.explanation: As the policy being trained changes, so does the distribution of trajectory lengths it produces — a length predictor calibrated early in training becomes unreliable later, which is why Libra's C-MLFQ scheduler instead uses causal signals from tool-return outcomes.

questions.prompt: On a 48 A800 GPU cluster, what throughput and convergence gains does the paper report for Libra versus baseline schedulers?
options: 1.2x throughput, no change in convergence speed
Up to 3.0x higher throughput and up to 2.5x faster reward convergence
10x throughput but slower convergence
No measurable throughput change, only lower memory usage
questions.explanation: The paper reports up to 3.0x higher throughput and up to 2.5x faster convergence in reward compared to baseline schedulers, evaluated on a 48 A800 GPU cluster.

references.title: Libra: Efficient Resource Management for Agentic RL Post-Training (arXiv:2606.03077)

posts.title: Claude Sonnet 5: The Agent Workhorse Gets an Upgrade
posts.subtitle: Not the smartest Claude — the one built to finish the whole job
posts.summary: Anthropic shipped Claude Sonnet 5 on June 30, 2026, closing much of the gap with Opus 4.8 on agentic and coding benchmarks while staying priced for high-volume agent workloads. The headline jump is reliability on long, multi-step tasks, not raw peak intelligence.

slides.body: On June 30, 2026, Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 5 — the newest entry in its mainstream Sonnet line, positioned as the model you run agents on all day, not just the one you reach for on the hardest problem.
slides.title: A Model Built to Finish, Not Just Answer

slides.body: Anthropic's own framing: a two-part enterprise task — update Salesforce account tiers, then send a launch announcement to enterprise contacts — used to stall halfway with Sonnet 4.6.
bullets: Long agent chains compound small errors across steps
Tool-use and terminal tasks were a particular weak point for Sonnet 4.6
Users needed Opus-tier reliability without Opus-tier cost for everyday agent work
slides.title: Multi-Step Agent Tasks Used to Stall

slides.body: The gains aren't evenly spread — they're concentrated exactly where agents fail most: tool use, terminal work, and computer control.
bullets: Terminal-bench: 76.1%, up from Sonnet 4.6's 55.4% — the single biggest jump, +20.7 points
OSWorld-Verified (computer use): 81.2%, up from 78.5%
GDPval-AA v2 (knowledge work): 1,618, edging out Opus 4.8's 1,615
Anthropic's safety testing found a lower rate of undesirable behaviors in agentic contexts than Sonnet 4.6
slides.title: What Actually Improved
Sonnet 5 vs. Sonnet 4.6 vs. Opus 4.8

slides.body: The same two-part CRM task — update account tiers across records, then draft and send an announcement to the right contact list — now completes without a human stepping in mid-way.
bullets: Sonnet 5 holds the full multi-step plan across the tool calls instead of losing the thread partway through
Both context window (1M tokens) and pricing are shared across Sonnet 4.6, Sonnet 5, and Opus 4.8 on the API
The intro price cuts cost roughly 33% versus standard pricing through August 31, 2026
slides.title: The Salesforce Task, End to End

slides.body: Sonnet 5 isn't trying to beat Opus 4.8 on raw difficulty — it's trying to make the 90% of agent work that doesn't need Opus-level reasoning cheaper and more reliable.
bullets: Opus 4.8 still leads on the hardest reasoning: 69.2% vs. Sonnet 5's 63.2% on the hardest agentic-coding split
Sonnet 5 essentially matches or beats Opus 4.8 on knowledge-work and computer-use tasks at roughly 40% of the cost
The right choice depends on the task's ceiling, not just its category
slides.title: Why a Mid-Tier Model, Not a Bigger One

bullets: Claude Sonnet 5 (June 30, 2026) targets agent reliability, especially tool use and terminal work
Terminal-bench jumped +20.7 points over Sonnet 4.6 — the largest single gain
It undercuts Opus 4.8 on price by roughly 60% while matching it on some knowledge-work benchmarks
Opus 4.8 still wins on the hardest reasoning — pick Sonnet 5 for volume, Opus for ceiling
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.prompt: What is Claude Sonnet 5's biggest reported benchmark jump over Sonnet 4.6?
options: SWE-bench Verified, +2 points
Terminal-bench, +20.7 points (55.4% to 76.1%)
A new multimodal video benchmark
Context window size
questions.explanation: Terminal-bench saw the largest jump, from 55.4% to 76.1% — a +20.7 point gain, reflecting a specific focus on tool-use and command-line agent reliability.

questions.prompt: On the hardest agentic-coding benchmark split, how does Sonnet 5 compare to Opus 4.8?
options: Sonnet 5 outperforms Opus 4.8 on every benchmark
Opus 4.8 still leads (69.2% vs. Sonnet 5's 63.2%), even though Sonnet 5 closed much of the prior gap
They are identical models with different names
Sonnet 5 is only available for computer-use tasks
questions.explanation: Opus 4.8 still holds the lead on the hardest agentic-coding tasks, meaning Sonnet 5 is best understood as closing the gap and improving reliability, not surpassing Opus 4.8 outright.

questions.prompt: Why would a team choose Claude Sonnet 5 over Opus 4.8 for a production agent pipeline?

posts.title: Hybrid Search: Why Vector-Only RAG Misses Exact Matches
posts.subtitle: Combining BM25 and embeddings without ever comparing their scores
posts.summary: Vector search alone misses exact-match queries — product codes, error IDs, rare terms — because embeddings encode meaning, not literal strings. Hybrid search runs BM25 and dense retrieval in parallel and fuses their rankings with Reciprocal Rank Fusion, which sidesteps the fact that a cosine score and a BM25 score aren't comparable numbers.

slides.body: Most RAG pipelines retrieve with vector similarity alone. That works for meaning — and quietly fails for exact strings.
slides.title: Two Search Engines Are Better Than One

slides.body: Dense embeddings are trained to capture semantic similarity, not literal string overlap. That makes them weak on the queries where an exact match matters most.
bullets: A query for error code 'E4521' can rank a doc mentioning it 8th, behind semantically-similar but wrong docs
Rare product IDs, acronyms, and named entities often get smoothed away in embedding space
Most RAG failures traced in production are retrieval failures, not hallucinations — the model never saw the right chunk
slides.title: Vector-Only RAG Has a Blind Spot

slides.body: BM25 is a decades-old lexical scoring formula: it rewards exact term overlap, weighted by how rare and how discriminative each term is.
bullets: BM25 (sparse/lexical): excellent at exact terms, IDs, rare words; blind to paraphrase and synonyms
Dense vectors (semantic): excellent at paraphrase and concept matches; weak on exact rare-term recall
Neither one dominates the other — they fail on different queries
slides.title: BM25 and Dense Vectors Are Complementary

slides.body: The obvious approach — average a 0.85 cosine score with a 12.4 BM25 score — breaks immediately, because the two scores live on unrelated scales. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) sidesteps this by throwing scores away and using only rank position.
bullets: RRF score for a document = sum over each retriever of 1 / (k + rank), typically k = 60
A document ranked #1 by BM25 and #5 by vectors scores higher than one ranked #3 and #40
No score normalization, no manual weight tuning between retrievers required
slides.title: Fusing Ranks, Not Scores

slides.body: Query: 'error code E4521 timeout'. BM25 ranks the doc containing the literal string 'E4521' at #1 because it's a rare, highly discriminative term. The dense retriever ranks the same doc #8, since 'E4521' looks like generic noise in embedding space but a doc about 'connection timeout errors' looks more semantically relevant.
bullets: A purely semantic doc that BM25 never surfaces (rank absent) only gets credit from the vector list
The exact-match doc wins the fused ranking without either retriever needing to be reweighted by hand
slides.title: Worked Example: An Error-Code Query

rows: Two indexes to build and maintain

slides.title: Retrieval Method, Head to Head

slides.body: Hybrid search adds real infrastructure cost — two indexes, two retrieval calls per query, and a fusion step to tune.
bullets: Small, homogeneous corpora (a few hundred FAQ docs) rarely need it — one method is usually enough
If your queries are almost never exact-match (pure conversational Q&A), dense-only may suffice
The k constant in RRF (commonly 60) still needs sanity-checking against your own query mix, not blind copy-paste
slides.title: When Hybrid Search Isn't Worth It

bullets: Vector-only RAG systematically underperforms on exact-match queries like IDs and rare terms
BM25 and dense retrieval fail in different, complementary ways — run both, not one
RRF fuses rankings using rank position only, avoiding the incompatible-score-scales problem
Skip hybrid search for small, low-exact-match corpora — the added infra isn't always worth it
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.prompt: Why does vector-only (dense embedding) retrieval often fail on queries containing exact IDs or rare technical terms?
options: Vector databases can't store short strings
Dense embeddings are trained to capture semantic similarity, not literal string overlap, so rare exact terms get smoothed away
Vector search is always slower than keyword search
Embeddings only work on English text
questions.explanation: Embeddings encode meaning, so a rare literal string like a product code or error ID doesn't stand out the way it does to a lexical method like BM25 — semantically 'similar' documents can outrank the one with the actual exact match.

questions.prompt: Why can't you just average a BM25 score and a cosine similarity score to combine rankings?
options: Averaging is computationally too expensive
The two scores live on different, incompatible scales, so a direct numeric combination is unreliable
BM25 scores are always negative
Cosine similarity cannot be computed for text
questions.explanation: A BM25 score (e.g. 12.4) and a cosine similarity (e.g. 0.85) aren't measuring the same thing on the same scale — naively averaging them lets whichever score happens to have a larger numeric range dominate the fusion.

questions.prompt: How does Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combine results from two retrievers?
options: It picks whichever retriever returned more results
It sums 1/(k + rank) for each document across each retriever's ranked list, using only rank position, not the underlying scores
It multiplies the raw scores from both retrievers together
It always trusts BM25 over vector search
questions.explanation: RRF discards raw scores entirely and computes a document's fused score from the reciprocal of its rank in each list (1/(k+rank), typically k=60), which sidesteps the score-incompatibility problem completely.

posts.title: iLLaDA: Diffusion Language Models Catch Up
posts.subtitle: An 8B bidirectional diffusion model narrows the gap with autoregressive LLMs
posts.summary: iLLaDA is an 8B-parameter masked diffusion language model trained with fully bidirectional attention instead of the usual left-to-right causal attention. Scaled to 12T pretraining tokens, it posts double-digit point gains over its predecessor on reasoning, math, and code benchmarks.

slides.title: Two Ways to Write a Sentence
slides.body: Almost every LLM you use writes left to right, one token at a time. iLLaDA writes differently — and a new paper shows that approach finally catching up.

slides.title: Autoregressive Models Have a Ceiling
slides.body: Causal, left-to-right generation is simple and scales well, but a model can never revise a word it already committed to, and it can only attend to what came before it.
bullets: Generation is strictly sequential
Each token can only see earlier tokens
Errors early in a response can't be corrected

slides.title: What Masked Diffusion Does Instead
slides.body: Diffusion language models start from a fully masked sequence and iteratively 'unmask' tokens in parallel, attending in both directions at once — closer to how diffusion image models denoise pixels.
bullets: Bidirectional attention, not causal
Tokens can be revised as more context appears
Generation happens in parallel steps, not strictly left-to-right

slides.title: What iLLaDA Changes
slides.body: iLLaDA keeps the masked-diffusion objective through both pretraining and fine-tuning, scales to 12T pretraining tokens, fine-tunes on a 25B-token instruction corpus, and adds variable-length generation plus confidence-based scoring for evaluation.
bullets: 12T tokens of masked-diffusion pretraining
25B-token instruction fine-tuning, 12 epochs
Variable-length generation for efficiency
Confidence-based scoring for multiple-choice evals

slides.title: The Gains, By Benchmark
Why This Matters
slides.body: Because diffusion LLMs generate non-sequentially, they open the door to parallel decoding and easier self-correction — things autoregressive models can't do as naturally.
bullets: Parallel token generation is a structural, not just engineering, advantage
Bidirectional context can catch inconsistencies earlier
Still trails top autoregressive models overall, but the gap is shrinking fast

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Diffusion LLMs generate via iterative unmasking, not left-to-right
iLLaDA scales the masked-diffusion recipe to 12T pretraining tokens
Gains of 14-22 points across BBH, ARC, MATH, and HumanEval vs. its predecessor
Diffusion is emerging as a real architectural alternative to causal attention

questions.prompt: What's the core architectural difference between iLLaDA and a standard autoregressive LLM like GPT?
options: iLLaDA uses bidirectional attention and generates via iterative unmasking, not strict left-to-right decoding
iLLaDA has no attention mechanism at all
iLLaDA only works on images, not text
iLLaDA uses a smaller vocabulary
questions.explanation: Masked diffusion models attend in both directions and refine a fully-masked sequence over multiple steps, instead of committing to one token at a time left-to-right.

questions.prompt: iLLaDA scaled its masked-diffusion pretraining to roughly how many tokens?
options: 12 million
12 billion
12 trillion
12 quadrillion
questions.explanation: The paper scales pretraining to 12T tokens, keeping the masked-diffusion objective throughout instead of switching to a causal objective.

questions.prompt: According to the paper, iLLaDA-Base's biggest reported gain over LLaDA-Base was on:
options: BBH, +21.6 points
MATH, +14.5 points
HumanEval, +16.5 points
ARC-Challenge, +21.6 points
questions.explanation: iLLaDA-Base improved by 21.6 points on BBH and 14.9 points on ARC-Challenge over LLaDA-Base.

slides.title: A Lab Bench Made of Software

slides.title: Research Tools Don't Talk to Each Other

posts.title: Mixture of Experts: Big Model, Small Compute Bill
posts.subtitle: How frontier LLMs got bigger without getting proportionally slower
posts.summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures split a model's feed-forward layers into many specialized 'experts' and route each token to only a few of them. That lets a model have far more total parameters while only activating a small fraction per token — more capacity without a proportional compute bill.

slides.title: One Model, Many Specialists
slides.body: Frontier models like Mixtral and DeepSeek-V3 pack huge parameter counts — but only use a sliver of them per token. Here's how.

slides.title: Bigger Models Cost More Per Token
slides.body: In a dense model, every parameter does work on every single token. Doubling model size roughly doubles the compute cost of every forward pass — capacity and cost are locked together.
bullets: Dense models: every parameter fires on every token
More capacity always means more compute per token
Scaling up gets expensive fast

slides.title: Split the Layer Into Experts
slides.body: Mixture-of-Experts replaces a single large feed-forward block with many smaller 'expert' networks. A lightweight router picks a handful of experts for each token.
bullets: Dozens of expert sub-networks per layer
A router scores experts and picks the top-k (often 1-2)
Only the chosen experts run for that token

slides.title: Sparse Activation, Not Sparse Capacity
slides.body: The model's total parameter count includes every expert, but only a small fraction activate per token — so you get the memory footprint of a huge model with the compute cost of a much smaller one.
bullets: Total params: sum of all experts (huge)
Active params per token: just the chosen few (small)
The router is trained end-to-end with the rest of the model

slides.title: Dense vs. MoE

rows: Simple, but cost scales with total size
More total capacity, similar per-token compute

slides.title: Where You've Seen It
slides.body: Mixtral 8x7B has 47B total parameters but activates only about 13B per token, routing each token to 2 of 8 experts per layer — while matching or beating much larger dense models on many benchmarks.
bullets: 8 experts per layer, top-2 routing
47B total params, ~13B active per token
Competitive with far larger dense models on several benchmarks

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: MoE decouples total capacity from per-token compute cost
A router picks a small top-k subset of experts per token
Memory footprint stays large even though compute per token shrinks
It's now a default lever for scaling frontier LLMs efficiently

questions.prompt: What problem does Mixture-of-Experts primarily solve?
options: It removes the need for attention layers
It decouples a model's total parameter capacity from its per-token compute cost
It eliminates the need for GPUs entirely
It compresses the vocabulary size
questions.explanation: MoE lets a model hold far more total parameters while only activating a small subset of them for each token, so capacity grows without a proportional rise in compute per token.

questions.prompt: In an MoE layer, what does the 'router' do?
options: It compresses the model's weights for storage
It scores the available experts and selects a small top-k subset to process each token
It schedules GPU jobs across a data center
It converts tokens into embeddings
questions.explanation: The router is a small learned network that decides which experts (usually just 1-2 out of many) should process each individual token.

questions.prompt: Why does an MoE model still need a lot of memory even though it activates few parameters per token?
options: Because all experts must be loaded even though only a few are used per token
Because MoE models store raw training data in memory
Because routers require more memory than the experts themselves
Because MoE models don't support quantization
questions.explanation: Every expert is a real set of weights that must be resident in memory to be available for routing, even though any single token only exercises a handful of them.

posts.title: KV Cache Quantization: 6x More Context for Free
posts.subtitle: How compressing an LLM's memory unlocks longer context without new hardware
posts.summary: KV cache quantization shrinks the memory an LLM uses to remember prior tokens, letting servers hold far more context per GPU without hurting accuracy. It's becoming a default lever in production inference stacks.

slides.title: The Hidden Memory Tax
slides.body: Every token an LLM generates re-reads every previous token's key and value vectors. That KV cache is often the single biggest consumer of GPU memory — bigger than the model weights.

slides.title: Context Is Expensive
slides.body: A 128K-token conversation can require tens of gigabytes of KV cache per request. More context means fewer concurrent users per GPU, or costly hardware upgrades.
bullets: KV cache grows linearly with context length
Stored in full 16-bit precision by default
Directly limits batch size and concurrency

slides.title: What Quantization Does
slides.body: Instead of storing each key/value number in 16 bits, quantization rounds it to a smaller number of bits (e.g. 3-4) while preserving the range it represents.
bullets: Trades numeric precision for memory savings
Applied per-channel or per-block to limit error
Decoded back to full precision just before attention math

slides.title: Why It (Mostly) Works
slides.body: KV cache values tend to cluster in a narrow range, so aggressive quantization loses very little signal — recent techniques report near-zero measured accuracy loss even at 3 bits.
bullets: Smart calibration keeps outlier values accurate
Attention scores are surprisingly tolerant of rounding
Combines with paged/chunked cache management

slides.title: Precision vs. Memory
Real-World Payoff
slides.body: Google's TurboQuant approach (2026) compresses KV cache to 3 bits with no measured accuracy loss, delivering roughly 6x memory reduction — so one GPU can serve far more long-context requests.
bullets: 6x more concurrent long-context sessions
Lower cost per token served
Stacks with speculative decoding and paged attention

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: KV cache, not weights, is often the real memory bottleneck
Quantizing it trades a sliver of precision for major capacity gains
3-4 bit KV cache quantization is becoming a production default
Pair it with batching and speculative decoding for compounding wins

questions.prompt: What part of an LLM's inference workload does KV cache quantization primarily target?
options: The model's trained weights
The stored key/value vectors from prior tokens
The tokenizer vocabulary
The final output logits
questions.explanation: KV cache holds the key/value vectors for every previous token so attention can reference them; quantization compresses that specific store, not the model weights.

questions.prompt: Why does aggressive KV cache quantization (e.g. 3-4 bits) often lose very little accuracy?
options: LLMs ignore most of the context anyway
KV values cluster in a narrow range, so rounding loses little signal
Quantization only applies to unused tokens
GPUs automatically correct rounding errors
questions.explanation: Key/value activations tend to have a narrow, predictable range, so lower-precision representations retain almost all the useful signal, especially with good calibration.

questions.prompt: What's the main practical benefit of shrinking KV cache memory?
options: Faster model training
Smaller download size for the app
More concurrent long-context requests per GPU
Elimination of hallucinations
questions.explanation: Less memory per request means a server can hold more simultaneous or longer conversations on the same hardware, directly cutting cost per token served.

posts.title: Why 74% of AI Agent Rollouts Get Rolled Back
posts.subtitle: Autonomy without guardrails is the #1 reason enterprise agents fail
posts.summary: As agent spending rockets toward $206B in 2026, most failed deployments trace back to missing governance, not model quality. Guardrails, scoped permissions, and human checkpoints are what separate agents that scale from agents that get pulled.

slides.title: The Agent Rollback Problem
slides.body: AI agent spending is projected to hit $206.5B in 2026 — but a growing share of deployments get built, launched, and then quietly shut off.

slides.title: It's Not the Model
slides.body: Teams assume agent failures are AI-quality problems. Usually they're control problems: nobody defined what the agent is allowed to touch, undo, or escalate.
bullets: Agents given broad, standing permissions
No audit trail for autonomous actions
No human checkpoint before high-stakes steps

slides.title: What Governance Means Here
slides.body: Agent governance is the set of rules, permissions, and review points that bound what an autonomous system can do — before it does something costly.
bullets: Scoped, least-privilege tool access
Human-in-the-loop for irreversible actions
Logging every decision and tool call

slides.title: Guardrails That Actually Work
slides.body: Enterprises that scale agents budget for external validation and staged autonomy instead of flipping agents to full autonomy on day one.
bullets: Start read-only, expand permissions gradually
Require approval above a cost/impact threshold
Continuously monitor for drift from intended behavior

slides.title: Rolled Back vs. Scaled
One Process at a Time
slides.body: Winning teams map a single messy workflow, add a human review step, and prove measurable time saved or errors reduced before expanding scope.
bullets: Pick one high-friction, low-blast-radius process
Instrument before-and-after metrics
Only widen scope once trust is earned

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Most agent failures are governance failures, not model failures
Least-privilege access and staged autonomy prevent costly surprises
Human checkpoints on irreversible actions build trust fast
Prove value on one narrow workflow before scaling out

questions.prompt: According to the trend, what is the most common reason enterprise AI agent deployments get rolled back?
options: The underlying model is too slow
Lack of governance and guardrails, not model quality
Agents are too expensive to run
Users don't understand AI
questions.explanation: Analyses point to missing governance — undefined permissions, no oversight — as the key differentiator between agents that scale and those that get pulled.

questions.prompt: What does 'least-privilege' mean in the context of AI agent tool access?
options: Giving the agent every available tool for flexibility
Granting only the minimum permissions needed for a specific task
Running the agent without any tools
Letting the agent request new permissions automatically
questions.explanation: Least-privilege scoping limits an agent to exactly the tools and data it needs, shrinking the blast radius if it misbehaves or is misused.

questions.prompt: What's a recommended first step when rolling out a new autonomous agent in production?
options: Give it full autonomy immediately to move fast
Start with read-only or low-impact actions and expand gradually
Skip logging to reduce latency
Deploy it across all workflows at once
questions.explanation: Staged autonomy — starting narrow and expanding only after trust is established — is what separates deployments that scale from ones that get rolled back.
